title: Do not hardcode secrets or long-lived credentials
text: Hardcoded tokens, passwords, API keys, and similar credentials create rotation, exposure, and audit problems. Prefer runtime-provided secrets with narrowly scoped access.
summary: Secret-like literals should live in secret management or deployment configuration, not in source.
embedding_text:
rule_no: SEC.1
pack_id: shared_security
language_id: shared
context_id: 
dialect_id: 
section: SEC
title: Do not hardcode secrets or long-lived credentials
summary: Secret-like literals should live in secret management or deployment configuration, not in source.
keywords: not, secrets, credentials, sec.1, hardcode, long-lived, secret-like, literals, should, live, secret, management
category: security
reviewability: auto_review
trigger_patterns: hardcoded_secret
fix_guidance: Move the secret into environment-backed configuration or a secret manager and inject it at runtime.
text: Hardcoded tokens, passwords, API keys, and similar credentials create rotation, exposure, and audit problems. Prefer runtime-provided secrets with narrowly scoped access.
fix_guidance: Move the secret into environment-backed configuration or a secret manager and inject it at runtime.

title: Build shell commands from trusted primitives, not concatenated strings
text: Command construction through string concatenation is fragile and exposes injection risk. Prefer argument vectors, explicit allow-lists, or dedicated APIs.
summary: Shell execution paths should not interpolate untrusted fragments into a command string.
embedding_text:
rule_no: SEC.2
pack_id: shared_security
language_id: shared
context_id: 
dialect_id: 
section: SEC
title: Build shell commands from trusted primitives, not concatenated strings
summary: Shell execution paths should not interpolate untrusted fragments into a command string.
keywords: shell, not, command, sec.2, build, commands, trusted, primitives, concatenated, strings, execution, paths
category: security
reviewability: auto_review
trigger_patterns: shell_injection
fix_guidance: Use structured subprocess or exec APIs that separate the executable from validated arguments.
text: Command construction through string concatenation is fragile and exposes injection risk. Prefer argument vectors, explicit allow-lists, or dedicated APIs.
fix_guidance: Use structured subprocess or exec APIs that separate the executable from validated arguments.

title: Parameterize SQL instead of composing query text dynamically
text: SQL should be parameterized through the database driver's bind facilities or a higher-level abstraction. Dynamic text assembly should be reserved for carefully controlled structural clauses.
summary: Query text built from variables is difficult to reason about and easy to misuse.
embedding_text:
rule_no: SEC.3

language_id: shared
context_id: 
dialect_id: 
section: SEC
title: Parameterize SQL instead of composing query text dynamically

keywords: text, sql, query, should, sec.3, parameterize, instead, composing, dynamically, built, variables, difficult
category: security
reviewability: auto_review
trigger_patterns: sql_injection, dynamic_sql
fix_guidance: Bind values separately from the SQL string and constrain structural query changes through allow-listed fragments.
text: SQL should be parameterized through the database driver's bind facilities or a higher-level abstraction. Dynamic text assembly should be reserved for carefully controlled structural clauses.
fix_guidance: Bind values separately from the SQL string and constrain structural query changes through allow-listed fragments.

title: Do not leave fallback secrets or test credentials in production code paths
text: Even when intended only for local development or bootstrap, embedded fallback secrets create a second and weaker trust path that is easy to forget during incident response or key rotation.
summary: Backup credentials often become the least-audited credential in the system.

category: security
reviewability: auto_review
trigger_patterns: hardcoded_secret
fix_guidance: Remove embedded fallback credentials and require runtime configuration or a dedicated local-only development path instead.
text: Even when intended only for local development or bootstrap, embedded fallback secrets create a second and weaker trust path that is easy to forget during incident response or key rotation.
fix_guidance: Remove embedded fallback credentials and require runtime configuration or a dedicated local-only development path instead.

title: Keep command execution APIs structured so the executable and arguments remain distinct
text: Separating the executable from validated arguments narrows the injection surface and makes the trust boundary visible in code review across languages and runtimes.
summary: Security review is much stronger when command construction never depends on ad hoc shell parsing.

text: Separating the executable from validated arguments narrows the injection surface and makes the trust boundary visible in code review across languages and runtimes.

title: Example, bootstrap, and fallback credentials in committed code should be treated as real secrets unless clearly impossible to activate
text: Backup and bootstrap secrets often become the least-audited authentication path in a system because everyone assumes they are temporary or unreachable.
summary: "Test" or "dev" labels do not remove rotation, misuse, or confusion risk once credentials are committed.

title: Kernel launches should keep the launch-failure and post-launch error boundary visible
text: A `<<<...>>>` launch hides whether launch failure is checked immediately, deferred to a later sync, or lost entirely behind a distant helper. Reviewers should still be able to see which boundary owns launch status and execution failure.
summary: CUDA launch syntax is asynchronous, so failure ownership becomes easy to lose in review.

pack_id: cuda_core
language_id: cuda
context_id: 
dialect_id: 
section: CUDA
title: Kernel launches should keep the launch-failure and post-launch error boundary visible
summary: CUDA launch syntax is asynchronous, so failure ownership becomes easy to lose in review.
keywords: launch, should, boundary, failure, cuda.1, kernel, launches, keep, launch-failure, post-launch, error, visible
category: error_handling
reviewability: auto_review
trigger_patterns: cuda_kernel_launch
fix_guidance: Keep launch-error handling or the owning synchronization/result boundary close enough to the launch that reviewers can see who observes failure.
text: A `<<<...>>>` launch hides whether launch failure is checked immediately, deferred to a later sync, or lost entirely behind a distant helper. Reviewers should still be able to see which boundary owns launch status and execution failure.
fix_guidance: Keep launch-error handling or the owning synchronization/result boundary close enough to the launch that reviewers can see who observes failure.

title: Device, pinned-host, and managed allocations should keep ownership and matching free explicit
text: `cudaMalloc`, `cudaHostAlloc`, and `cudaMallocManaged` make lifetime, transfer ownership, and teardown easy to hide behind helper code. The review should still reveal who frees the allocation and when that free is safe relative to outstanding work.
summary: CUDA allocation APIs create memory with non-local lifetime and synchronization implications.

context_id: 
dialect_id:

summary: CUDA allocation APIs create memory with non-local lifetime and synchronization implications.
keywords: should, ownership, free, allocation, lifetime, cuda.2, device, pinned-host, managed, allocations, keep, matching
category: resource_management
reviewability: auto_review
trigger_patterns: cuda_malloc, cuda_managed_memory
fix_guidance: Keep allocation ownership visible, pair it with the matching free path, and avoid burying transfer of responsibility behind ambiguous helper APIs.
text: `cudaMalloc`, `cudaHostAlloc`, and `cudaMallocManaged` make lifetime, transfer ownership, and teardown easy to hide behind helper code. The review should still reveal who frees the allocation and when that free is safe relative to outstanding work.
fix_guidance: Keep allocation ownership visible, pair it with the matching free path, and avoid burying transfer of responsibility behind ambiguous helper APIs.

title: `__syncthreads()` must not sit under lane-dependent control flow
text: A block-level barrier inside a branch keyed by `threadIdx`, lane identity, or another per-thread condition can deadlock or produce undefined behavior because some threads skip the barrier while others wait forever.
summary: A barrier is only safe when every thread in the block reaches it under the same control-flow contract.
embedding_text:

pack_id: cuda_core
language_id: cuda
context_id: 
dialect_id: 
section: CUDA
title: `__syncthreads()` must not sit under lane-dependent control flow
summary: A barrier is only safe when every thread in the block reaches it under the same control-flow contract.
keywords: barrier, under, cuda.3, syncthreads, must, not, sit, lane-dependent, control, flow, only, safe
category: correctness
reviewability: auto_review
trigger_patterns: cuda_divergent_syncthreads
fix_guidance: Move the barrier out of divergent control flow or rewrite the algorithm so every thread in the block reaches the same synchronization point.
text: A block-level barrier inside a branch keyed by `threadIdx`, lane identity, or another per-thread condition can deadlock or produce undefined behavior because some threads skip the barrier while others wait forever.
fix_guidance: Move the barrier out of divergent control flow or rewrite the algorithm so every thread in the block reaches the same synchronization point.

title: Device-wide or broad stream synchronization should be exceptional in steady-state paths
text: `cudaDeviceSynchronize`, `cudaStreamSynchronize`, and event synchronization can serialize unrelated work or hide where completion is actually owned. Review whether the synchronization scope is wider than the local contract requires.
summary: Broad synchronization is often a hidden latency and concurrency boundary, not just a debugging helper.

category: performance
reviewability: auto_review
trigger_patterns: cuda_device_sync
fix_guidance: Narrow the synchronization scope, move completion handling to the true owner, or document why a broad sync is the intended contract.
text: `cudaDeviceSynchronize`, `cudaStreamSynchronize`, and event synchronization can serialize unrelated work or hide where completion is actually owned. Review whether the synchronization scope is wider than the local contract requires.
fix_guidance: Narrow the synchronization scope, move completion handling to the true owner, or document why a broad sync is the intended contract.

title: Default-stream launches and copies should be intentional rather than accidental serialization
text: A kernel launch or `cudaMemcpy` on the default stream may serialize work that callers expected to overlap. That is especially reviewable when the patch mixes synchronous copies and launches without a clearly owned stream boundary.
summary: The default stream is a runtime contract that can silently change overlap and ordering semantics.

language_id: cuda

category: concurrency
reviewability: auto_review
trigger_patterns: cuda_kernel_launch, cuda_memcpy_sync
fix_guidance: Pass an explicit stream where overlap matters or document that default-stream sequencing is the intended ordering contract.
text: A kernel launch or `cudaMemcpy` on the default stream may serialize work that callers expected to overlap. That is especially reviewable when the patch mixes synchronous copies and launches without a clearly owned stream boundary.
fix_guidance: Pass an explicit stream where overlap matters or document that default-stream sequencing is the intended ordering contract.

title: `__host__ __device__` helpers should not hide host-only or device-only assumptions
text: A helper compiled for host and device can drift when it performs logging, allocation, synchronization, or control-flow that only one side really supports. Review whether the function contract is still valid in both environments.
summary: Dual-annotated helpers stay maintainable only when both execution contexts share the same visible contract.

language_id: cuda

category: correctness
reviewability: auto_review
trigger_patterns: cuda_host_device
fix_guidance: Split host and device behavior when their contracts differ, or keep the shared helper limited to logic that is truly valid in both compilation targets.
text: A helper compiled for host and device can drift when it performs logging, allocation, synchronization, or control-flow that only one side really supports. Review whether the function contract is still valid in both environments.
fix_guidance: Split host and device behavior when their contracts differ, or keep the shared helper limited to logic that is truly valid in both compilation targets.

title: Shared-memory sizing and launch-geometry assumptions should stay explicit at the kernel boundary
text: `__shared__`, `extern __shared__`, and launch-bounds annotations couple kernel code to launch geometry and byte-size math. Reviewers should be able to see whether the launch site still matches the kernel's shared-memory assumptions.
summary: Shared-memory bugs often come from a launch contract that is no longer obvious in the local patch.

title: Managed memory in latency-sensitive paths should make residency and migration assumptions explicit
text: `cudaMallocManaged` can be correct and still surprise the caller with page migration or residency behavior that is invisible in the patch. Review whether the subsystem owns those migration assumptions explicitly.
summary: Managed memory is easier to adopt than to reason about when latency or overlap matters.

fix_guidance: Document the residency expectation, prefetch or advise when needed, or keep latency-sensitive code on an ownership model with clearer movement semantics.
text: `cudaMallocManaged` can be correct and still surprise the caller with page migration or residency behavior that is invisible in the patch. Review whether the subsystem owns those migration assumptions explicitly.
fix_guidance: Document the residency expectation, prefetch or advise when needed, or keep latency-sensitive code on an ownership model with clearer movement semantics.

title: Atomic-heavy hot paths should justify their contention or reduction strategy
text: An `atomicAdd` or similar primitive in a hot kernel can quietly turn scalable work into one-address serialization. Review whether the patch still has a credible reduction, sharding, or contention story.
summary: Atomics are correct only when the resulting contention profile is still acceptable for the workload.

pack_id: cuda_performance
language_id: cuda
context_id:

summary: Atomics are correct only when the resulting contention profile is still acceptable for the workload.

category: performance
reviewability: auto_review
trigger_patterns: cuda_atomic
fix_guidance: Prefer block-local reduction, sharded accumulation, or another design that keeps a single global atomic hotspot from dominating the kernel.
text: An `atomicAdd` or similar primitive in a hot kernel can quietly turn scalable work into one-address serialization. Review whether the patch still has a credible reduction, sharding, or contention story.
fix_guidance: Prefer block-local reduction, sharded accumulation, or another design that keeps a single global atomic hotspot from dominating the kernel.

title: Warp-lane-dependent branches in hot kernels should be intentional
text: Branches keyed directly from `threadIdx`, lane ID, or warp-size modulo logic may be correct and still fragment warp progress. Review whether the divergence is essential and bounded rather than accidental.
summary: Lane-dependent control flow is often the shortest path to warp divergence and unstable throughput.

category: performance
reviewability: auto_review
trigger_patterns: cuda_warp_divergence
fix_guidance: Reshape the work so warp lanes follow more uniform control flow, or keep the divergent path clearly isolated and intentionally small.
text: Branches keyed directly from `threadIdx`, lane ID, or warp-size modulo logic may be correct and still fragment warp progress. Review whether the divergence is essential and bounded rather than accidental.
fix_guidance: Reshape the work so warp lanes follow more uniform control flow, or keep the divergent path clearly isolated and intentionally small.

title: Async copies and stream-local work should preserve stream ownership and overlap assumptions
text: `cudaMemcpyAsync` and related stream-local work can look non-blocking while still depending on an implicit stream or completion boundary that reviewers cannot see from the patch.
summary: Asynchronous transfer APIs help only when the completion and stream contract are still visible.

category: concurrency

fix_guidance: Keep the owning stream explicit and make the completion boundary or dependent work visible near the transfer site.
text: `cudaMemcpyAsync` and related stream-local work can look non-blocking while still depending on an implicit stream or completion boundary that reviewers cannot see from the patch.
fix_guidance: Keep the owning stream explicit and make the completion boundary or dependent work visible near the transfer site.

title: Async CUDA APIs that explicitly target stream 0 should justify default-stream ordering
text: An explicit 0 stream argument on cudaMemcpyAsync-style APIs often means the code still relies on default-stream ordering even though the call shape looks asynchronous. Review whether that serialization is intentional or just missing stream plumbing.
summary: Explicit stream 0 keeps async-looking code inside NULL-stream ordering semantics.

category: concurrency

trigger_patterns: cuda_async_default_stream
fix_guidance: Route the work through an explicit owned stream when overlap matters, or document that NULL-stream ordering is the intended contract.
text: An explicit 0 stream argument on cudaMemcpyAsync-style APIs often means the code still relies on default-stream ordering even though the call shape looks asynchronous. Review whether that serialization is intentional or just missing stream plumbing.
fix_guidance: Route the work through an explicit owned stream when overlap matters, or document that NULL-stream ordering is the intended contract.

title: Stream host callbacks should not hide buffer or object lifetime beyond the stream boundary
text: cudaLaunchHostFunc or callback-based stream hooks can make host or device buffer lifetime harder to reason about because the host work runs only after earlier stream work completes, often after the local stack frame has moved on.
summary: Host callbacks run later than the local scope that launched them, so captured ownership can become ambiguous.

category: correctness
reviewability: auto_review
trigger_patterns: cuda_stream_callback
fix_guidance: Keep callback captures narrow, make ownership explicit, or move post-processing behind a component that already owns the stream and buffer lifetimes.
text: cudaLaunchHostFunc or callback-based stream hooks can make host or device buffer lifetime harder to reason about because the host work runs only after earlier stream work completes, often after the local stack frame has moved on.
fix_guidance: Keep callback captures narrow, make ownership explicit, or move post-processing behind a component that already owns the stream and buffer lifetimes.

title: Creating CUDA streams inside hot loops should justify lifecycle churn and ownership
text: When a loop creates CUDA streams repeatedly, reviewers need to see whether the code truly needs per-iteration stream churn or is missing a longer-lived owner, pool, or reuse boundary.
summary: Per-iteration stream creation is rarely free and often signals missing long-lived stream ownership.

category: performance
reviewability: auto_review
trigger_patterns: cuda_stream_create_in_loop
fix_guidance: Reuse or pool long-lived streams when the lifecycle is not truly per-iteration, and keep create and destroy ownership visible.
text: When a loop creates CUDA streams repeatedly, reviewers need to see whether the code truly needs per-iteration stream churn or is missing a longer-lived owner, pool, or reuse boundary.
fix_guidance: Reuse or pool long-lived streams when the lifecycle is not truly per-iteration, and keep create and destroy ownership visible.

title: Pipeline async copies should not feed staged data before a visible completion boundary
text: Async pipeline copies can overlap transfer and compute, but the local patch still needs to show where the staged tile becomes safe to read. If compute starts consuming the staged shared-memory buffer before a visible `consumer_wait`, `wait_prior`, `arrive_and_wait`, or equivalent completion boundary, reviewers cannot tell whether the copy has actually completed.
summary: `cuda::memcpy_async`, `__pipeline_memcpy_async`, and `cp.async` stay reviewable only when the first staged-data use is clearly behind a wait boundary.
embedding_text:
rule_no: CUDA.PIPE.1
pack_id: cuda_pipeline_async
language_id: cuda
context_id: 
dialect_id: 
section: CUDAPIPE
title: Pipeline async copies should not feed staged data before a visible completion boundary
summary: `cuda::memcpy_async`, `__pipeline_memcpy_async`, and `cp.async` stay reviewable only when the first staged-data use is clearly behind a wait boundary.
keywords: staged, pipeline, async, copies, before, visible, completion, boundary, compute, cuda.pipe.1, should, not
category: correctness
reviewability: auto_review
trigger_patterns: cuda_pipeline_copy_without_wait
fix_guidance: Keep the first staged-data use adjacent to an explicit wait boundary, or wrap the copy and wait sequence in a helper whose readiness contract is obvious at the call site.
text: Async pipeline copies can overlap transfer and compute, but the local patch still needs to show where the staged tile becomes safe to read. If compute starts consuming the staged shared-memory buffer before a visible `consumer_wait`, `wait_prior`, `arrive_and_wait`, or equivalent completion boundary, reviewers cannot tell whether the copy has actually completed.
fix_guidance: Keep the first staged-data use adjacent to an explicit wait boundary, or wrap the copy and wait sequence in a helper whose readiness contract is obvious at the call site.

title: Pipeline producer and consumer phase operations should not sit under rank-dependent control flow
text: When only a subset of threads performs pipeline acquire, commit, wait, or release operations, producer and consumer phase ownership can drift away from the participant set that actually issued or consumes the staged copy. That makes the phase contract much harder to audit and can break the intended collective pipeline behavior.
summary: `producer_acquire`, `producer_commit`, `consumer_wait`, and `consumer_release` are collective phase ownership points, not lane-local optimizations.
embedding_text:
rule_no: CUDA.PIPE.2
pack_id: cuda_pipeline_async
language_id: cuda
context_id: 
dialect_id: 
section: CUDAPIPE
title: Pipeline producer and consumer phase operations should not sit under rank-dependent control flow
summary: `producer_acquire`, `producer_commit`, `consumer_wait`, and `consumer_release` are collective phase ownership points, not lane-local optimizations.
keywords: phase, pipeline, producer, consumer, operations, not, collective, ownership, can, cuda.pipe.2, should, sit
category: correctness
reviewability: auto_review
trigger_patterns: cuda_pipeline_phase_ops_in_branch
fix_guidance: Move pipeline phase operations to a control-flow region reached by every participating thread, then branch only after the phase boundary is complete and locally obvious.
text: When only a subset of threads performs pipeline acquire, commit, wait, or release operations, producer and consumer phase ownership can drift away from the participant set that actually issued or consumes the staged copy. That makes the phase contract much harder to audit and can break the intended collective pipeline behavior.
fix_guidance: Move pipeline phase operations to a control-flow region reached by every participating thread, then branch only after the phase boundary is complete and locally obvious.

title: Barrier-backed pipeline phases should keep participant scope and phase parity explicit
text: `cuda::barrier`, `mbarrier`, and `arrive_and_wait` can synchronize staged async copies, but the barrier phase only stays interpretable when reviewers can see who participates in each arrival and wait. If barrier operations sit under partial-participation branches, the local code hides whether barrier parity still matches the async-copy contract.
summary: Async barrier arrival and wait are only safe when the same participant set owns the bound copy and the barrier phase transition.
embedding_text:
rule_no: CUDA.PIPE.3
pack_id: cuda_pipeline_async
language_id: cuda
context_id: 
dialect_id: 
section: CUDAPIPE
title: Barrier-backed pipeline phases should keep participant scope and phase parity explicit
summary: Async barrier arrival and wait are only safe when the same participant set owns the bound copy and the barrier phase transition.
keywords: barrier, phase, participant, parity, async, arrival, only, when, can, cuda.pipe.3, barrier-backed, pipeline
category: concurrency
reviewability: auto_review
trigger_patterns: cuda_pipeline_barrier_in_branch
fix_guidance: Keep barrier arrivals and waits in a full-participant region, or document the exact participant scope and bound-copy ownership at the barrier boundary.
text: `cuda::barrier`, `mbarrier`, and `arrive_and_wait` can synchronize staged async copies, but the barrier phase only stays interpretable when reviewers can see who participates in each arrival and wait. If barrier operations sit under partial-participation branches, the local code hides whether barrier parity still matches the async-copy contract.
fix_guidance: Keep barrier arrivals and waits in a full-participant region, or document the exact participant scope and bound-copy ownership at the barrier boundary.

title: Cluster-wide synchronization must not sit under non-uniform participation
text: Calling `cluster.sync()` or an equivalent cluster barrier path from control flow keyed by block rank, thread rank, or another non-uniform predicate can break the cluster-wide collective contract just as surely as a divergent block barrier breaks `__syncthreads()`.
summary: Cluster synchronization is a collective contract across the cluster, not a per-block convenience API.
embedding_text:
rule_no: CUDA.CLUSTER.1
pack_id: cuda_thread_block_cluster
language_id: cuda
context_id: 
dialect_id: 
section: CUDACLUSTER
title: Cluster-wide synchronization must not sit under non-uniform participation
summary: Cluster synchronization is a collective contract across the cluster, not a per-block convenience API.
keywords: cluster, cluster-wide, synchronization, not, non-uniform, collective, contract, barrier, block, rank, cuda.cluster.1, must
category: correctness
reviewability: auto_review
trigger_patterns: cuda_cluster_sync_in_branch
fix_guidance: Move cluster-wide synchronization into a full-participant region that every thread in the cluster reaches, or use a narrower synchronization primitive if only one block should participate.
text: Calling `cluster.sync()` or an equivalent cluster barrier path from control flow keyed by block rank, thread rank, or another non-uniform predicate can break the cluster-wide collective contract just as surely as a divergent block barrier breaks `__syncthreads()`.
fix_guidance: Move cluster-wide synchronization into a full-participant region that every thread in the cluster reaches, or use a narrower synchronization primitive if only one block should participate.

title: Distributed shared-memory access should wait for a visible cluster-wide readiness boundary
text: `map_shared_rank()` exposes another block's shared memory inside the same cluster, but that does not remove the need for a visible cluster-wide readiness boundary before remote shared state is consumed or updated. If remote DSM access appears before a visible `cluster.sync()` or equivalent cluster barrier, reviewers cannot tell whether the producing block has reached the required initialization phase.
summary: Remote shared memory is only reviewable when the local code still shows which cluster-wide boundary makes the producer block's data ready.
embedding_text:
rule_no: CUDA.CLUSTER.2
pack_id: cuda_thread_block_cluster
language_id: cuda
context_id: 
dialect_id: 
section: CUDACLUSTER
title: Distributed shared-memory access should wait for a visible cluster-wide readiness boundary
summary: Remote shared memory is only reviewable when the local code still shows which cluster-wide boundary makes the producer block's data ready.
keywords: visible, cluster-wide, boundary, remote, shared, block, access, readiness, memory, cluster, before, cuda.cluster.2
category: concurrency
reviewability: auto_review
trigger_patterns: cuda_cluster_dsm_without_sync
fix_guidance: Synchronize the cluster before the first remote DSM use, and keep the remote rank and readiness boundary close enough that the ownership contract is locally obvious.
text: `map_shared_rank()` exposes another block's shared memory inside the same cluster, but that does not remove the need for a visible cluster-wide readiness boundary before remote shared state is consumed or updated. If remote DSM access appears before a visible `cluster.sync()` or equivalent cluster barrier, reviewers cannot tell whether the producing block has reached the required initialization phase.
fix_guidance: Synchronize the cluster before the first remote DSM use, and keep the remote rank and readiness boundary close enough that the ownership contract is locally obvious.

title: Cluster-dependent kernels should keep the cluster launch contract explicit
text: Code that uses `this_cluster()`, `cluster_group`, `map_shared_rank()`, or `cluster.sync()` is depending on a cluster launch contract that does not exist for an ordinary kernel launch. When the same local file shows a triple-chevron launch but no `__cluster_dims__` attribute or explicit cluster launch API, reviewers cannot reconstruct whether the cluster requirement is actually owned by the launch path.
summary: If kernel correctness depends on cluster APIs or DSM, the local code should also show how the kernel is actually launched as a cluster.

language_id: cuda

dialect_id: 
section: CUDACLUSTER
title: Cluster-dependent kernels should keep the cluster launch contract explicit
summary: If kernel correctness depends on cluster APIs or DSM, the local code should also show how the kernel is actually launched as a cluster.
keywords: cluster, launch, kernel, should, contract, explicit, local, code, actually, cuda.cluster.3, cluster-dependent, kernels
category: correctness
reviewability: auto_review
trigger_patterns: cuda_cluster_launch_without_contract
fix_guidance: Keep the cluster dimension contract explicit near the kernel definition or launch site, for example with `__cluster_dims__` or an explicit cluster launch configuration path.
text: Code that uses `this_cluster()`, `cluster_group`, `map_shared_rank()`, or `cluster.sync()` is depending on a cluster launch contract that does not exist for an ordinary kernel launch. When the same local file shows a triple-chevron launch but no `__cluster_dims__` attribute or explicit cluster launch API, reviewers cannot reconstruct whether the cluster requirement is actually owned by the launch path.
fix_guidance: Keep the cluster dimension contract explicit near the kernel definition or launch site, for example with `__cluster_dims__` or an explicit cluster launch configuration path.

title: TMA bulk tensor copies should not feed shared tiles before a visible completion boundary
text: TMA bulk tensor copies can overlap tensor movement with compute, but the local patch still needs to show where the destination shared tile becomes safe to read. If the code reaches `consume_tile`, `use_tile`, or another first tile-consumption step before a visible `bar.wait`, `mbarrier` wait, or equivalent completion boundary, reviewers cannot tell whether the tensor transfer has actually completed.
summary: `cp_async_bulk_tensor` and `cp.async.bulk.tensor` stay reviewable only when the first shared-tile use is clearly behind the matching barrier wait.
embedding_text:
rule_no: CUDA.TMA.1
pack_id: cuda_tma
language_id: cuda
context_id: 
dialect_id: 
section: CUDATMA
title: TMA bulk tensor copies should not feed shared tiles before a visible completion boundary
summary: `cp_async_bulk_tensor` and `cp.async.bulk.tensor` stay reviewable only when the first shared-tile use is clearly behind the matching barrier wait.
keywords: tensor, tma, bulk, copies, shared, before, visible, completion, boundary, first, cuda.tma.1, should
category: correctness
reviewability: auto_review
trigger_patterns: cuda_tma_copy_without_wait
fix_guidance: Keep the first shared-tile use adjacent to the barrier wait that closes the TMA transfer, or wrap the copy and wait sequence in a helper whose readiness contract is obvious at the call site.
text: TMA bulk tensor copies can overlap tensor movement with compute, but the local patch still needs to show where the destination shared tile becomes safe to read. If the code reaches `consume_tile`, `use_tile`, or another first tile-consumption step before a visible `bar.wait`, `mbarrier` wait, or equivalent completion boundary, reviewers cannot tell whether the tensor transfer has actually completed.
fix_guidance: Keep the first shared-tile use adjacent to the barrier wait that closes the TMA transfer, or wrap the copy and wait sequence in a helper whose readiness contract is obvious at the call site.

title: Tensor map inputs should keep the transport contract explicit
text: TMA code often relies on tensor maps that were encoded on the host and transported into device code through a specific ownership path. When the local code uses `cp_async_bulk_tensor` with a raw `CUtensorMap* tensor_map` path but does not show `__grid_constant__`, a constant-memory transport path, or an explicit tensor-map fence, reviewers cannot reconstruct whether the descriptor is stable and visible to the consuming thread block.
summary: `CUtensorMap` ownership is hard to audit when a TMA path reads it from a raw pointer without a visible `__grid_constant__` or proxy-fence contract.
embedding_text:
rule_no: CUDA.TMA.2
pack_id: cuda_tma
language_id: cuda
context_id: 
dialect_id: 
section: CUDATMA
title: Tensor map inputs should keep the transport contract explicit
summary: `CUtensorMap` ownership is hard to audit when a TMA path reads it from a raw pointer without a visible `__grid_constant__` or proxy-fence contract.
keywords: path, code, tensor, transport, explicit, cutensormap, ownership, when, tma, raw, visible, grid_constant__
category: correctness
reviewability: auto_review
trigger_patterns: cuda_tma_tensor_map_pointer_without_contract
fix_guidance: Prefer a `const __grid_constant__ CUtensorMap` kernel parameter for steady-state maps, or keep the global-memory acquisition and required fence visible near the first TMA use.
text: TMA code often relies on tensor maps that were encoded on the host and transported into device code through a specific ownership path. When the local code uses `cp_async_bulk_tensor` with a raw `CUtensorMap* tensor_map` path but does not show `__grid_constant__`, a constant-memory transport path, or an explicit tensor-map fence, reviewers cannot reconstruct whether the descriptor is stable and visible to the consuming thread block.
fix_guidance: Prefer a `const __grid_constant__ CUtensorMap` kernel parameter for steady-state maps, or keep the global-memory acquisition and required fence visible near the first TMA use.

title: On-device tensor-map mutation should publish the updated descriptor through a visible proxy fence
text: Tensor-map mutation helpers such as `tensormap_replace_*` can update descriptor state on the device, but that mutation stays hard to trust unless the local code also shows the proxy-fence or publication step before later TMA instructions reuse the updated map. If a patch mutates a tensor map and then issues `cp_async_bulk_tensor` without a visible `tensormap_cp_fenceproxy`, `tensormap_copy_fenceproxy`, or equivalent publication path, reviewers cannot tell whether the descriptor update is actually visible to the TMA consumer.
summary: `tensormap_replace*` changes descriptor state, so later TMA use should also show the proxy-fence step that makes the update visible.

dialect_id:

title: On-device tensor-map mutation should publish the updated descriptor through a visible proxy fence
summary: `tensormap_replace*` changes descriptor state, so later TMA use should also show the proxy-fence step that makes the update visible.
keywords: descriptor, mutation, visible, tma, update, tensor-map, should, updated, state, later, also, proxy-fence
category: concurrency
reviewability: auto_review
trigger_patterns: cuda_tma_tensormap_replace_without_fence
fix_guidance: Keep the proxy-fence or descriptor publication step adjacent to the tensor-map mutation, and make the later TMA use depend on that visible contract.
text: Tensor-map mutation helpers such as `tensormap_replace_*` can update descriptor state on the device, but that mutation stays hard to trust unless the local code also shows the proxy-fence or publication step before later TMA instructions reuse the updated map. If a patch mutates a tensor map and then issues `cp_async_bulk_tensor` without a visible `tensormap_cp_fenceproxy`, `tensormap_copy_fenceproxy`, or equivalent publication path, reviewers cannot tell whether the descriptor update is actually visible to the TMA consumer.
fix_guidance: Keep the proxy-fence or descriptor publication step adjacent to the tensor-map mutation, and make the later TMA use depend on that visible contract.

title: Repeated cudaSetDevice inside loops should keep current-device ownership explicit
text: A loop that repeatedly calls cudaSetDevice can make allocation, stream, and error ownership depend on ambient current-device state rather than explicit per-device handles. Review whether the loop truly owns that device switch boundary.
summary: Current-device mutation is ambient process state, so loop-local switching is easy to misuse.
embedding_text:

context_id: 
dialect_id:

title: Repeated cudaSetDevice inside loops should keep current-device ownership explicit
summary: Current-device mutation is ambient process state, so loop-local switching is easy to misuse.
keywords: current-device, cudasetdevice, ownership, explicit, ambient, state, loop, cuda.mg.1, repeated, inside, loops, should
category: correctness
reviewability: auto_review
trigger_patterns: cuda_set_device_in_loop
fix_guidance: Move device scoping to a higher-level owner or make per-device state explicit so later edits do not depend on hidden current-device assumptions.
text: A loop that repeatedly calls cudaSetDevice can make allocation, stream, and error ownership depend on ambient current-device state rather than explicit per-device handles. Review whether the loop truly owns that device switch boundary.
fix_guidance: Move device scoping to a higher-level owner or make per-device state explicit so later edits do not depend on hidden current-device assumptions.

title: Peer access and peer copies should keep topology and fallback assumptions explicit
text: Peer access enablement and peer copies create topology-dependent behavior that can quietly differ across hosts or partitions. Review whether capability checks, ordering, and fallback paths are still explicit enough for the deployment target.
summary: Multi-GPU fast paths are only safe when capability checks and fallback boundaries remain visible.

trigger_patterns: cuda_peer_access
fix_guidance: Keep topology checks, enablement boundaries, and non-peer fallbacks explicit so the data-movement contract stays reviewable.
text: Peer access enablement and peer copies create topology-dependent behavior that can quietly differ across hosts or partitions. Review whether capability checks, ordering, and fallback paths are still explicit enough for the deployment target.
fix_guidance: Keep topology checks, enablement boundaries, and non-peer fallbacks explicit so the data-movement contract stays reviewable.

title: NCCL collective launch order and stream ownership should stay globally obvious
text: NCCL collectives and group boundaries are concise to write but depend on consistent communicator, stream, and ordering assumptions across ranks and sometimes across communicators. Review whether those assumptions are still explicit from the local code.
summary: A local NCCL call participates in a cross-rank ordering contract that small edits can still break.

text: NCCL collectives and group boundaries are concise to write but depend on consistent communicator, stream, and ordering assumptions across ranks and sometimes across communicators. Review whether those assumptions are still explicit from the local code.
fix_guidance: Keep communicator ownership, stream association, and grouped collective ordering obvious enough that cross-rank behavior can still be reasoned about locally.

title: WGMMA issue should remain warpgroup-uniform
text: Issuing `wgmma.mma_async` from control flow keyed by lane id, warp id, or another non-uniform predicate can break the warpgroup participation contract just as surely as a divergent warp-collective Tensor Core path. If only part of the warpgroup reaches the issue site, reviewers cannot trust that the async MMA contract is still satisfied.
summary: `wgmma.mma_async` is a warpgroup collective, not a lane-local fast path.

category: correctness
reviewability: auto_review
trigger_patterns: cuda_wgmma_issue_in_branch
fix_guidance: Move WGMMA issue sites into a control-flow region reached by the whole warpgroup, then branch only after the collective issue boundary is complete and locally obvious.
text: Issuing `wgmma.mma_async` from control flow keyed by lane id, warp id, or another non-uniform predicate can break the warpgroup participation contract just as surely as a divergent warp-collective Tensor Core path. If only part of the warpgroup reaches the issue site, reviewers cannot trust that the async MMA contract is still satisfied.
fix_guidance: Move WGMMA issue sites into a control-flow region reached by the whole warpgroup, then branch only after the collective issue boundary is complete and locally obvious.

title: WGMMA commit and wait ownership should not hide under rank-dependent control flow
text: WGMMA issue is only half of the contract; commit-group, wait-group, and fence operations decide when outstanding async MMA work becomes visible. When those operations sit under lane- or warp-dependent control flow, reviewers cannot tell whether the same participant set that issued the work is also closing the async group.
summary: `wgmma.commit_group`, `wgmma.wait_group`, and warpgroup fences close async work, so their participant set should stay explicit.

reviewability: auto_review
trigger_patterns: cuda_wgmma_group_ops_in_branch
fix_guidance: Keep WGMMA commit and wait operations in a warpgroup-uniform region, or make the exact participant scope explicit at the local boundary.
text: WGMMA issue is only half of the contract; commit-group, wait-group, and fence operations decide when outstanding async MMA work becomes visible. When those operations sit under lane- or warp-dependent control flow, reviewers cannot tell whether the same participant set that issued the work is also closing the async group.
fix_guidance: Keep WGMMA commit and wait operations in a warpgroup-uniform region, or make the exact participant scope explicit at the local boundary.

title: Accumulator or epilogue use should wait for a visible WGMMA completion boundary
text: WGMMA decouples issue from completion, so the local patch still needs to show where dependent accumulator or epilogue work becomes safe. If the code narrows results, stores an epilogue, or otherwise consumes the accumulator before a visible `wgmma.wait_group`, reviewers cannot reconstruct whether the outstanding async MMA work has actually completed.
summary: `wgmma.wait_group` should remain visibly ahead of the first accumulator or epilogue use that depends on the outstanding async MMA work.

text: WGMMA decouples issue from completion, so the local patch still needs to show where dependent accumulator or epilogue work becomes safe. If the code narrows results, stores an epilogue, or otherwise consumes the accumulator before a visible `wgmma.wait_group`, reviewers cannot reconstruct whether the outstanding async MMA work has actually completed.
fix_guidance: Place the first accumulator-dependent epilogue step directly behind `wgmma.wait_group`, or wrap the wait and epilogue in a helper whose completion contract is obvious at the call site.

title: Tensor Core warp collectives must keep full-warp participation explicit
text: `wmma::load_matrix_sync`, `wmma::mma_sync`, `wmma::store_matrix_sync`, and low-level tensor-core instructions such as `mma.sync` or `ldmatrix` are warp-collective operations. If a lane-dependent branch controls who reaches them, the kernel can become undefined or silently corrupt results.
summary: WMMA and warp-level matrix instructions are only safe when all participating lanes reach the same collective operation.
embedding_text:
rule_no: CUDA.TC.1

language_id: cuda

title: Tensor Core warp collectives must keep full-warp participation explicit
summary: WMMA and warp-level matrix instructions are only safe when all participating lanes reach the same collective operation.
keywords: instructions, cuda.tc.1, tensor, core, warp, collectives, must, keep, full-warp, participation, explicit, wmma
category: correctness
reviewability: auto_review
trigger_patterns: cuda_tensor_core_divergent_collective
fix_guidance: Move tensor-core collectives out of lane-dependent branches or make the predicate warp-uniform so every participating lane reaches the same collective instruction.
text: `wmma::load_matrix_sync`, `wmma::mma_sync`, `wmma::store_matrix_sync`, and low-level tensor-core instructions such as `mma.sync` or `ldmatrix` are warp-collective operations. If a lane-dependent branch controls who reaches them, the kernel can become undefined or silently corrupt results.
fix_guidance: Move tensor-core collectives out of lane-dependent branches or make the predicate warp-uniform so every participating lane reaches the same collective instruction.

title: Inline Tensor Core PTX should keep shape, alignment, and staging contracts obvious
text: Inline PTX for Tensor Core kernels often bakes in matrix shape, memory layout, and alignment assumptions that are much harder to audit than the high-level algorithm. Reviewers should still be able to tell which shared-memory staging and operand contract the inline instruction expects.
summary: `mma.sync` and `ldmatrix` fast paths are brittle because correctness depends on layout, alignment, and tile-shape assumptions that are easy to hide.

category: correctness
reviewability: auto_review
trigger_patterns: cuda_tensor_inline_ptx
fix_guidance: Keep the tensor-core instruction shape, operand layout, and staging assumptions explicit near the asm block, or wrap them in a helper whose contract is documented at the call site.
text: Inline PTX for Tensor Core kernels often bakes in matrix shape, memory layout, and alignment assumptions that are much harder to audit than the high-level algorithm. Reviewers should still be able to tell which shared-memory staging and operand contract the inline instruction expects.
fix_guidance: Keep the tensor-core instruction shape, operand layout, and staging assumptions explicit near the asm block, or wrap them in a helper whose contract is documented at the call site.

title: Mixed-precision Tensor Core epilogues should make narrowing and accumulator ownership explicit
text: Tensor Core code often multiplies low-precision inputs but accumulates into a wider type such as `float`. That can still lose correctness when the epilogue quietly narrows results back to `half`, `bfloat16`, or another reduced-precision output without making the numerical boundary obvious in the local patch.
summary: Low-precision Tensor Core kernels are maintainable only when the patch still shows where wider accumulation is narrowed back to a smaller output type.

language_id: cuda

title: Mixed-precision Tensor Core epilogues should make narrowing and accumulator ownership explicit
summary: Low-precision Tensor Core kernels are maintainable only when the patch still shows where wider accumulation is narrowed back to a smaller output type.
keywords: tensor, core, low-precision, when, still, wider, back, output, cuda.tc.3, mixed-precision, epilogues, should
category: correctness
reviewability: auto_review
trigger_patterns: cuda_tensor_mixed_precision_boundary
fix_guidance: Keep the accumulation type and the final narrowing boundary explicit, and make any saturation, rounding, or range assumption visible near the epilogue that stores the result.
text: Tensor Core code often multiplies low-precision inputs but accumulates into a wider type such as `float`. That can still lose correctness when the epilogue quietly narrows results back to `half`, `bfloat16`, or another reduced-precision output without making the numerical boundary obvious in the local patch.
fix_guidance: Keep the accumulation type and the final narrowing boundary explicit, and make any saturation, rounding, or range assumption visible near the epilogue that stores the result.

title: Cooperative-group partitioning must not happen under non-uniform control flow
text: Functions such as `tiled_partition`, `labeled_partition`, and `binary_partition` require all threads in the parent group to participate consistently. Creating those groups only from a lane-dependent branch can deadlock or corrupt subsequent collective behavior.
summary: Group creation is a collective contract, not a per-lane convenience API.

pack_id: cuda_cooperative_groups
language_id: cuda
context_id:

summary: Group creation is a collective contract, not a per-lane convenience API.
keywords: not, group, collective, cuda.cg.1, cooperative-group, partitioning, must, happen, under, non-uniform, control, flow
category: correctness
reviewability: auto_review
trigger_patterns: cuda_cg_partition_in_branch
fix_guidance: Create cooperative groups from a control-flow region that every participating thread reaches, then branch on subgroup rank only after the collective group exists.
text: Functions such as `tiled_partition`, `labeled_partition`, and `binary_partition` require all threads in the parent group to participate consistently. Creating those groups only from a lane-dependent branch can deadlock or corrupt subsequent collective behavior.
fix_guidance: Create cooperative groups from a control-flow region that every participating thread reaches, then branch on subgroup rank only after the collective group exists.

title: Grid-wide cooperative-group synchronization should keep cooperative launch ownership explicit
text: A `grid.sync()` call implies a cooperative launch contract that depends on grid size, residency, and launch API choices outside the local line. Reviewers should still be able to tell which boundary owns that launch requirement and whether all blocks that must participate are guaranteed to be resident together.
summary: `grid.sync()` is only valid when launch shape and residency assumptions are already owned by the caller.

fix_guidance: Keep the cooperative-launch requirement visible near the kernel launch or document the launch contract in the surrounding abstraction so `grid.sync()` is not an ambient assumption.
text: A `grid.sync()` call implies a cooperative launch contract that depends on grid size, residency, and launch API choices outside the local line. Reviewers should still be able to tell which boundary owns that launch requirement and whether all blocks that must participate are guaranteed to be resident together.
fix_guidance: Keep the cooperative-launch requirement visible near the kernel launch or document the launch contract in the surrounding abstraction so `grid.sync()` is not an ambient assumption.

title: Cooperative-group synchronization should not sit under rank-dependent control flow
text: Calling `cg::sync(group)` or `group.sync()` from a branch keyed by thread rank, lane identity, or another non-uniform predicate can break the collective contract of the group just as surely as a divergent `__syncthreads()` breaks a block barrier.
summary: Group sync has the same collective safety requirement as any other barrier in the group.
